mission.title: Install and Configure Continuous Optimization on Kubernetes
mission.description: Kraken is a chaos and resiliency testing tool for Kubernetes that helps improve performance under failure conditions. Installing Kraken allows you to simulate failures in your Kubernetes cluster to ensure it can withstand turbulent conditions.

steps.title: Add the Kraken Helm repository
steps.description:
First, add the Kraken Helm repository to your local Helm setup with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add krkn https://krkn-chaos.github.io/krkn-helm/
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Kraken using Helm
steps.description:
Now, install Kraken in your Kubernetes cluster using Helm. Replace `<namespace>` with your desired namespace:
```bash
helm install krkn krkn/krkn --namespace <namespace> --create-namespace --version v5.0.0
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the Kraken pods are running successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace <namespace>
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits for Kraken
steps.description:
To ensure Kraken operates efficiently, you may want to set resource limits. Edit the deployment:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment krkn --namespace <namespace>
```
Add resource limits under the `spec.containers` section:
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    memory: "512Mi"
    cpu: "500m"
```

steps.title: Set up RBAC for Kraken
steps.description:
Create a Role and RoleBinding to grant Kraken the necessary permissions:
```bash
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  namespace: <namespace>
  name: krkn-role
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods", "pods/log"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
EOF

kubectl create rolebinding krkn-rolebinding --role=krkn-role --serviceaccount=<namespace>:default --namespace=<namespace>
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Kraken will show the Kraken pods running in your specified namespace. You should also have resource limits and RBAC configured to ensure optimal performance and security.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add krkn https://krkn-chaos.github.io/krkn-helm/
helm repo update

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Kraken release from your Kubernetes cluster using Helm. This will remove all associated resources except for CRDs and persistent volumes.
```bash
helm uninstall krkn --namespace <namespace>
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Delete any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent resources that were created during the installation. This ensures a clean removal of Kraken from your cluster.
```bash
kubectl delete crd krkns.krkn.io
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace <namespace>
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
If you created a new namespace for Kraken, you can delete it along with all its resources. Verify that the namespace has been removed successfully.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace <namespace>
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's a good practice to back up the current configuration and state of Kraken. You can export the current Helm release configuration.
```bash
helm get values krkn --namespace <namespace> > krkn-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository to ensure you have the latest charts, then upgrade Kraken to the desired version. Replace `<new-version>` with the version you want to upgrade to.
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade krkn krkn/krkn --namespace <namespace> --version <new-version>
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After upgrading, check that the Kraken pods are running with the new version. This ensures that the upgrade was successful.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace <namespace>
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the Kraken pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for any errors that might indicate the cause of the failure.
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace <namespace>
```

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources
troubleshooting.description:
If you receive errors related to insufficient resources, ensure that your cluster has enough CPU and memory available. You can check the resource usage with:
```bash
kubectl top pods --namespace <namespace>
```

troubleshooting.title: RBAC permissions issues
troubleshooting.description:
If Kraken is unable to perform actions due to RBAC permissions, verify that the Role and RoleBinding are correctly set up. You can check the current roles with:
```bash
kubectl get role --namespace <namespace>
kubectl get rolebinding --namespace <namespace>
```

troubleshooting.title: Helm release not found
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter an error stating that the Helm release is not found, ensure that you are using the correct namespace and release name. List all releases in the namespace with:
```bash
helm list --namespace <namespace>
```

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and Helm installed on your local machine.
